pathways.label: General conviction sealing under N.D.C.C. chapter 12-60.1
pathways.summary: A person may petition to seal a North Dakota criminal record if they: Conviction Sealing Under N.D.C.C. Chapter 12-60.1 Conviction Type Basic Eligibility Rule Misdemeanor conviction Guilty plea or guilty finding, and no new conviction for at least 3 years before filing Felony conviction Guilty plea or guilty finding, and no new conviction for at least 5 years before filing

ruleClauses: A person may petition to seal a North Dakota criminal record if they:
Conviction Sealing Under N.D.C.C. Chapter 12-60.1
Conviction Type Basic Eligibility Rule
Misdemeanor conviction Guilty plea or guilty finding, and no new conviction for at least 3 years before filing
Felony conviction Guilty plea or guilty finding, and no new conviction for at least 5 years before filing
Unconditional pardon Governor granted an unconditional pardon
These are the main eligibility grounds in N.D.C.C. 12-60.1-02. (
"https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
Was this a misdemeanor conviction, felony conviction, or a case that ended without a conviction?
Important conviction-sealing limits
Chapter 12-60.1 does not apply to:
Certain felony offenses involving violence or intimidation during the firearm-disability period.
Any offense requiring registration under N.D.C.C. 12.1-32-15, including offenders against children and sexual offenders.
DUI records, which have their own separate sealing statute. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
Wilma should not mark these as eligible without attorney review.
Nonconviction Court Record Closing
North Dakota created a newer process for nonconviction records under N.D.C.C. 12-60.1-05.
means all criminal charges in the case were dismissed or the person was acquitted of all criminal charges. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
If the nonconviction order was entered on or after August 1, 2025
The court must close the court record 61 days after the order of nonconviction. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
If all charges were dismissed or you were acquitted on or after August 1, 2025, the court record should close automatically after 61 days, unless an exception applies.
If the nonconviction order was entered before August 1, 2025

The defendant may file a Petition to Close Nonconviction Records. If the requirements are met, the court must enter an order closing the court record within 10 days of the petition being filed, and no filing fee may be charged. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
This pathway does not apply if:
The dismissal was part of a plea agreement involving conviction on another offense.
The dismissal was due to a finding that the person was not fit to proceed.
The not-guilty verdict was due to lack of criminal responsibility.
The case was appealed. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
waitingRules: A hearing cannot be held earlier than 45 days after the petition is filed, unless the prosecutor stipulates to sealing without a hearing or on a faster schedule. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
2 They were not later found guilty of another DUI or any other criminal offense within 7 years of the first DUI violation.

DUI is a special North Dakota pathway. Do not use the general criminal-record sealing process. We need to check the exact DUI statute, whether seven years passed with no later criminal offense, and whether you had or have a commercial drivers license.

If a person pleads guilty or is found guilty of a first offense involving possession of one ounce or less of marijuana or two grams or less of THC, and judgment is entered, the court, on motion, must seal the court record if the person is not subsequently convicted within two years of a further controlled-substance violation. Once sealed, the court record may not be opened even by court order. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t19c03-1.pdf")
Was this a first offense for possession of one ounce or less of marijuana or two grams or less of THC, and did you avoid another controlled-substance conviction for two years?

North Dakota also has a Summary Pardon Application for certain marijuana offenses. The Pardon Advisory Board says people prosecuted and convicted of misdemeanor marijuana possession, marijuana ingestion, or marijuana paraphernalia who have had no other criminal convictions in the past five years may submit a Summary Pardon Application. Felony convictions are not eligible for the summary marijuana application and must use the regular pardon application. ("https://www.docr.nd.gov/pardon-advisory-board?utm_source=chatgpt.com")

pathways.label: DUI-record sealing under the separate DUI statute
pathways.summary: DUI records cannot be sealed through the general Chapter 12-60.1 process. They use N.D.C.C. 39-08-01.6. A person may ask a North Dakota municipal or state court to seal a DUI record if: 1 They pled guilty, pled nolo contendere, or were found guilty of violating N.D.C.C. 39-08-01 or an equivalent municipal ordinance; and 2 They were not later found guilty of another DUI or any other criminal offense within 7 years of the first DUI violation. A person licensed as a commercial driver is excluded from this DUI sealing statute. Prosecutors may still access prior DUI records for enhancement purposes.

Marijuana sealing and marijuana summary pardon are not the same thing. One is a court-record sealing process; the other is an executive pardon process.
Human-Trafficking-Victim Vacatur and Sealing

A person convicted of prostitution or certain listed offenses committed as a direct result of being a human-trafficking victim may move to vacate the conviction and seal the record. Official victim documentation is not required, but official documentation from a federal, state, local, or tribal agency creates a presumption that the offense participation was a direct result of being a victim. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12-1c41.pdf")

Covered listed offenses include prostitution, misdemeanor forgery, misdemeanor theft, insufficient funds or credit offenses, manufacture or possession of controlled or counterfeit substances, and drug paraphernalia offenses when tied to trafficking victimization. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12-1c41.pdf")
Wilma should escalate these cases to a lawyer or trafficking-survivor legal clinic.
Juvenile Records, DNA Profiles, and Unconstitutional Arrests
North Dakota court guidance identifies additional limited expungement categories:
Juvenile unruly/delinquency records Retention/destruction rules apply; agencies respond that no record exists after destruction
DNA profiles Petition may be available where the underlying arrest did not result in a felony charge/conviction or was dismissed/acquitted/reversed
Unconstitutional arrests Expungement may be available if arrest was unconstitutional and charges were dismissed or conviction overturned
These are limited, specialized pathways and should be escalated if raised.

Path D - Deferred Imposition of Sentence / Dismissal

North Dakota allows a court to defer imposition of sentence and place the person on probation. If the person fulfills probation conditions, the court may allow withdrawal of the guilty plea, set aside the guilty verdict, dismiss the charge, and release the person from penalties and disabilities, except for certain registration/firearm consequences. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12-1c32.pdf")

When imposition of sentence is deferred and the guilty plea is withdrawn or the guilty verdict is set aside under 12.1-32-07.1, the records and papers are generally accessible only to the clerk, judge, juvenile commissioner, probation officers, the defendant or defense counsel, and the states attorney; others need a written court order. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12-1c32.pdf")

sourceSections.title: Core North Dakota Rule
sourceSections.text:
In North Dakota, sealing generally means prohibiting disclosure of the existence or contents of court or prosecution records unless authorized by court order. North Dakotas sealing chapter defines
criminal record
as court and prosecution records, and expressly excludes certain criminal-history information and criminal-justice data systems. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
Wilma should explain it this way:

North Dakota usually offers sealing, not full expungement. Sealing can limit access to court and prosecution records, but it may not delete records or remove every record held by BCI, law enforcement, federal systems, private background-check companies, or licensing agencies.

North Dakota court guidance also warns that certain records reported to the Bureau of Criminal Investigation / BCIcannot be sealed under Chapter 12-60.1, including criminal history record information and criminal justice data information in the Criminal Justice Data Information Sharing System.

sourceSections.title: Main North Dakota Pathways
sourceSections.text:
Path A - Conviction Sealing Under N.D.C.C. Chapter 12-60.1
A person may petition to seal a North Dakota criminal record if they:

Conviction Type Basic Eligibility Rule
Misdemeanor conviction Guilty plea or guilty finding, and no new conviction for at least 3 years before filing
Felony conviction Guilty plea or guilty finding, and no new conviction for at least 5 years before filing
Unconditional pardon Governor granted an unconditional pardon
These are the main eligibility grounds in N.D.C.C. 12-60.1-02. (

"https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
Wilma should ask:

Was this a misdemeanor conviction, felony conviction, or a case that ended without a conviction?

Important conviction-sealing limits
Chapter 12-60.1 does not apply to:
Certain felony offenses involving violence or intimidation during the firearm-disability period.
Any offense requiring registration under N.D.C.C. 12.1-32-15, including offenders against children and sexual offenders.
DUI records, which have their own separate sealing statute. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
Wilma should not mark these as eligible without attorney review.

Path B - Nonconviction Court Record Closing
North Dakota created a newer process for nonconviction records under N.D.C.C. 12-60.1-05.
A
nonconviction
means all criminal charges in the case were dismissed or the person was acquitted of all criminal charges. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
If the nonconviction order was entered on or after August 1, 2025
The court must close the court record 61 days after the order of nonconviction. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
Wilma should say:

If all charges were dismissed or you were acquitted on or after August 1, 2025, the court record should close automatically after 61 days, unless an exception applies.

If the nonconviction order was entered before August 1, 2025
The defendant may file a Petition to Close Nonconviction Records. If the requirements are met, the court must enter an order closing the court record within 10 days of the petition being filed, and no filing fee may be charged. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
Nonconviction exceptions
This pathway does not apply if:
The dismissal was part of a plea agreement involving conviction on another offense.
The dismissal was due to a finding that the person was not fit to proceed.
The not-guilty verdict was due to lack of criminal responsibility.
The case was appealed. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
Important: the North Dakota petition form says the order closes only records controlled by the North Dakota court system; it does not close records controlled by the prosecutor or law-enforcement entities. ("https://www.ndcourts.gov/Media/Default/legal-resources/legal-self-help/other-forms/Close-Nonconviction-Records.pdf")

Path C - DUI Record Sealing
DUI records cannot be sealed through the general Chapter 12-60.1 process. They use N.D.C.C. 39-08-01.6.
A person may ask a North Dakota municipal or state court to seal a DUI record if:
1 They pled guilty, pled nolo contendere, or were found guilty of violating N.D.C.C. 39-08-01 or an equivalent municipal ordinance; and
2 They were not later found guilty of another DUI or any other criminal offense within 7 years of the first DUI violation.
A person licensed as a commercial driver is excluded from this DUI sealing statute. Prosecutors may still access prior DUI records for enhancement purposes.
Wilma should say:

DUI is a special North Dakota pathway. Do not use the general criminal-record sealing process. We need to check the exact DUI statute, whether seven years passed with no later criminal offense, and whether you had or have a commercial drivers license.

Path D - Deferred Imposition of Sentence / Dismissal
North Dakota allows a court to defer imposition of sentence and place the person on probation. If the person fulfills probation conditions, the court may allow withdrawal of the guilty plea, set aside the guilty verdict, dismiss the charge, and release the person from penalties and disabilities, except for certain registration/firearm consequences. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12-1c32.pdf")
When imposition of sentence is deferred and the guilty plea is withdrawn or the guilty verdict is set aside under 12.1-32-07.1, the records and papers are generally accessible only to the clerk, judge, juvenile commissioner, probation officers, the defendant or defense counsel, and the states attorney; others need a written court order. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12-1c32.pdf")
Wilma should ask:

Was this a deferred imposition of sentence, and did the court already dismiss the case after successful completion?

If the person only says
deferred,
Wilma must not assume the case is sealed or dismissed. Ask for the final order.

Path E - Marijuana-Specific Relief
North Dakota has two different marijuana-related pathways.

sourceSections.title: Court sealing for first offense possession
sourceSections.text:
If a person pleads guilty or is found guilty of a first offense involving possession of one ounce or less of marijuana or two grams or less of THC, and judgment is entered, the court, on motion, must seal the court record if the person is not subsequently convicted within two years of a further controlled-substance violation. Once sealed, the court record may not be opened even by court order. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t19c03-1.pdf")
Wilma should ask:

Was this a first offense for possession of one ounce or less of marijuana or two grams or less of THC, and did you avoid another controlled-substance conviction for two years?

sourceSections.title: Summary pardon for eligible marijuana offenses
sourceSections.text:
North Dakota also has a Summary Pardon Application for certain marijuana offenses. The Pardon Advisory Board says people prosecuted and convicted of misdemeanor marijuana possession, marijuana ingestion, or marijuana paraphernalia who have had no other criminal convictions in the past five years may submit a Summary Pardon Application. Felony convictions are not eligible for the summary marijuana application and must use the regular pardon application. ("https://www.docr.nd.gov/pardon-advisory-board?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
A pardon recommendation is not official until signed by the Governor. ("https://www.docr.nd.gov/pardon-advisory-board?utm_source=chatgpt.com")
Wilma should distinguish:

Marijuana sealing and marijuana summary pardon are not the same thing. One is a court-record sealing process; the other is an executive pardon process.

Path F - Human-Trafficking-Victim Vacatur and Sealing
A person convicted of prostitution or certain listed offenses committed as a direct result of being a human-trafficking victim may move to vacate the conviction and seal the record. Official victim documentation is not required, but official documentation from a federal, state, local, or tribal agency creates a presumption that the offense participation was a direct result of being a victim. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12-1c41.pdf")
Covered listed offenses include prostitution, misdemeanor forgery, misdemeanor theft, insufficient funds or credit offenses, manufacture or possession of controlled or counterfeit substances, and drug paraphernalia offenses when tied to trafficking victimization. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12-1c41.pdf")
Wilma should escalate these cases to a lawyer or trafficking-survivor legal clinic.

Path G - Juvenile Records, DNA Profiles, and Unconstitutional Arrests
North Dakota court guidance identifies additional limited expungement categories:

Category Rule
Juvenile unruly/delinquency records Retention/destruction rules apply; agencies respond that no record exists after destruction
DNA profiles Petition may be available where the underlying arrest did not result in a felony charge/conviction or was dismissed/acquitted/reversed
Unconstitutional arrests Expungement may be available if arrest was unconstitutional and charges were dismissed or conviction overturned
These are limited, specialized pathways and should be escalated if raised.

sourceSections.title: Conviction Sealing Requirements
sourceSections.text:
For a general conviction-sealing petition under Chapter 12-60.1, Wilma should collect enough facts to determine whether the user can prove:
Good cause for sealing.
Benefit to the petitioner outweighs the presumption of openness.
All terms of imprisonment and probation are complete.
All restitution has been paid.
Reformation/rehabilitation warrants relief.
Petition complies with Chapter 12-60.1. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
The court considers:
Nature and severity of the offense.
Risk to society.
Time since the offense.
Rehabilitation.
Aggravating and mitigating factors.
Criminal record.
Employment history.
Community involvement.
Recommendations from law enforcement, prosecutors, corrections officials, people familiar with the petitioner, and victims. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
A hearing cannot be held earlier than 45 days after the petition is filed, unless the prosecutor stipulates to sealing without a hearing or on a faster schedule. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")

sourceSections.title: Filing Process - General Conviction Sealing
sourceSections.text:
Step 1 - Confirm the case type
Wilma should classify:
Misdemeanor conviction.
Felony conviction.
DUI conviction.
Nonconviction.
Deferred imposition/dismissal.
Marijuana first-offense possession.
Human-trafficking-related conviction.
Juvenile/DNA/unconstitutional arrest issue.
Federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state case.
Step 2 - File in the existing criminal case
A petition to seal must be filed in the existing criminal case for the offense. The existing case must be a North Dakota municipal court case or North Dakota state district court case. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
Step 3 - Prepare required documents
At minimum, the filing should include:
Petition to Seal Criminal Records under Chapter 12-60.1.
Proposed Order to Seal Criminal Records.
Proof of service on the prosecuting attorney.
Step 4 - Include required petition information
The petition must include:
Full name and all other legal names or aliases.
Addresses from the date of the offense to the petition date.
Reasons why sealing should be granted.
Criminal history in North Dakota, other states, federal court, and foreign countries.
All prior and pending criminal charges.
All prior and pending deferred/stayed/continued-for-dismissal matters.
Prior requests for pardon, return of arrest records, expungement, or sealing in any forum, whether granted or denied. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
Step 5 - Serve prosecutor
The petition and proposed order must be served on the prosecuting official. The prosecutor is expected, to the extent practicable, to notify and seek input from law enforcement, witnesses, victims, and correctional authorities. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")
Step 6 - Hearing / court decision
The court may hold a hearing no earlier than 45 days after filing. The petitioner must prove the sealing factors by clear and convincing evidence. If denied, the court may prohibit refiling for up to one year for good cause. ("https://ndlegis.gov/cencode/t12c60-1.pdf")

sourceSections.title: Filing Process - Nonconviction Closing
sourceSections.text:
Use the Petition to Close Nonconviction Records if the case was fully dismissed or fully acquitted before August 1, 2025 and no statutory exception applies. The petition is filed with the clerk of court where the criminal case is filed, there is no filing fee, and the judge may require service on the prosecutor. ("https://www.ndcourts.gov/Media/Default/legal-resources/legal-self-help/other-forms/Close-Nonconviction-Records.pdf")
If the nonconviction order was entered on or after August 1, 2025, Wilma should tell the user they generally do not need to file anything; the court should close the court record after 61 days. ("https://www.ndcourts.gov/Media/Default/legal-resources/legal-self-help/other-forms/Close-Nonconviction-Records.pdf")
Access to closed nonconviction court records is limited to the clerk, judge, juvenile commission, criminal justice agency, defendant, defendants lawyer, states attorney, or a person with a written judges order. ("https://www.ndcourts.gov/Media/Default/legal-resources/legal-self-help/other-forms/Close-Nonconviction-Records.pdf")

sourceSections.title: Filing Process - DUI Sealing
sourceSections.text:
For DUI sealing, Wilma should route separately:
1 Confirm the DUI was under N.D.C.C. 39-08-01 or an equivalent municipal ordinance.
2 Confirm the person pled guilty, pled nolo contendere, or was found guilty.
3 Confirm no later DUI or any other criminal offense within 7 years.
4 Confirm the person is not a commercial driver.
5 File a written petition in the existing DUI case with the municipal or district court clerk.
A hearing is not required, but the judge may choose to hold one.

sourceSections.title: Wilma Intake Script
sourceSections.text:
Opening

I can help you understand North Dakota record-clearing options. North Dakota mostly uses sealing or closing records, not broad expungement. This is legal information, not legal advice. Eligibility depends on the exact court record, charge, outcome, sentence completion, restitution, and whether the record is held by the court, prosecutor, law enforcement, BCI, or another agency.

Core Questions
1 Was this case in North Dakota municipal court or North Dakota state district court?
2 Was it federal, tribal, military, or from another state?
3 What county and court handled the case?
4 What is the case number?
5 Was the case:
Conviction?
Dismissal?
Acquittal?
Deferred imposition?
DUI?
Marijuana possession?
Human-trafficking-related?
Juvenile?
DNA profile issue?
Unconstitutional arrest?
6 What was the exact charge and statute?
7 Was it a misdemeanor, felony, infraction, or DUI?
8 Was the person ordered to register as a sex offender or offender against children?
9 Was the felony violent or intimidating?
10 Was there a firearm-disability issue?
11 When was the conviction entered?
12 When were all imprisonment/probation terms completed?
13 Was all restitution paid?
14 Has the person had any new convictions?
15 Are there pending charges now?
16 Has the person ever received a pardon?
17 Was the person a commercial driver if the case was DUI?
18 Does the person need relief for employment, housing, licensing, immigration, school, firearms, or a professional background check?

sourceSections.title: Wilma Decision Tree
sourceSections.text:
If federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state
Route:

North Dakota state court relief likely cannot clear this directly. The user needs jurisdiction-specific review.

If all charges dismissed or acquitted
Route:

Check nonconviction closing under 12-60.1-05. If order was on or after August 1, 2025, court should close after 61 days. If before August 1, 2025, use Petition to Close Nonconviction Records if no exception applies.

If dismissal was part of plea deal
Route:

Nonconviction closing under 12-60.1-05 may not apply if dismissal was part of a plea agreement involving conviction on another offense.

If deferred imposition
Route:

Check whether the court entered final discharge, allowed withdrawal of plea or set aside verdict, and dismissed the case. If yes, records may be restricted under 12.1-32-07.2. If no, gather final-order status.

If misdemeanor conviction
Route:

Check Chapter 12-60.1: no new conviction for at least 3 years before filing, sentence/probation complete, restitution paid, no registration bar, and not DUI.

If felony conviction
Route:

Check Chapter 12-60.1: no new conviction for at least 5 years before filing, sentence/probation complete, restitution paid, no registration bar, and no violent/intimidating felony during firearm-disability period.

If DUI
Route:

Use 39-08-01.6, not Chapter 12-60.1. Check seven-year clean period, no other criminal offense, and no commercial-driver exclusion.

If first marijuana possession / THC possession
Route:

Check 19-03.1-23(9): first offense, one ounce or less marijuana or two grams or less THC, no subsequent controlled-substance conviction within two years.

If marijuana possession / ingestion / paraphernalia with no convictions in past five years
Route:

Also check Summary Pardon Application for eligible marijuana offenses.

If human-trafficking victimization
Route:

Possible motion to vacate and seal under 12.1-41-14. Escalate to attorney or survivor legal clinic.

sourceSections.title: Mandatory Attorney Escalation Flags
sourceSections.text:
Wilma should recommend attorney review if any of these apply:
Felony conviction.
Felony involving violence or intimidation.
Firearm-disability issue.
Sex offender or offender-against-children registration.
DUI with commercial drivers license.
DUI with any later criminal offense within 7 years.
Human-trafficking survivor vacatur.
Immigration consequences.
Federal, tribal, military, or out-of-state record.
Professional licensing or statutory background-check issue.
BCI record issue.
Prosecutor, victim, law-enforcement, or correctional objection.
Restitution unpaid.
Pending charge.
Deferred imposition status unclear.
Dismissal tied to plea agreement.
Lack-of-criminal-responsibility or fitness-to-proceed issue.
User wants a guaranteed outcome.

11. Wilma
Do Not Say
Rules
Wilma should not say:

North Dakota will erase your record.

Nobody can ever see it again.

This removes BCI records.

This removes FBI records.

This fixes immigration.

This restores gun rights.

DUI uses the normal sealing process.

A dismissal automatically disappears.

A deferred case is automatically cleared
without checking the final order.

A pardon always removes the conviction.

Private background-check companies will automatically update.

Wilma may say:

Based on what you shared, you may have a North Dakota sealing, closing, deferred-dismissal, marijuana, or pardon pathway. The next step is to verify the official court record, final disposition, sentence completion, restitution, new-conviction history, and which agency holds the record.

sourceSections.title: Filing Packet Checklist
sourceSections.text:
General Chapter 12-60.1 sealing packet
Collect:
Full legal name.
All aliases or prior legal names.
Date of birth.
Current address.
Address history from offense date to petition date.
Court name and county.
Case number.
Charge name and statute.
Offense level.
Conviction date.
Sentence completion date.
Probation completion proof.
Restitution payment proof.
New-conviction check for 3-year or 5-year period.
Full criminal history, including North Dakota, other states, federal court, and foreign countries.
Pending charges.
Deferred/stayed/continued-for-dismissal cases.

sourceSections.title: North Dakota Quick Reference
sourceSections.text:
Topic North Dakota Rule
Broad expungement Not generally available
Main conviction relief Sealing under N.D.C.C. Chapter 12-60.1
What
seal
means Limits disclosure of court/prosecution records unless court order allows access
Misdemeanor waiting period No new conviction for at least 3 years before filing
Felony waiting period No new conviction for at least 5 years before filing
Unconditional pardon Can support sealing petition
General filing location Existing criminal case
Petition documents Petition, proposed order, proof of service
Burden Clear and convincing evidence
Hearing timing Not earlier than 45 days after filing, unless prosecutor stipulates
Registration offenses Barred from Chapter 12-60.1
Violent/intimidating felonies Barred during firearm-disability period
DUI Separate 39-08-01.6 pathway
DUI clean period No later DUI or any other criminal offense within 7 years
CDL DUI issue Commercial drivers excluded
Nonconviction after Aug. 1, 2025 Court closes after 61 days
Nonconviction before Aug. 1, 2025 Petition; court closes within 10 days if eligible; no filing fee
Deferred imposition Records restricted after plea withdrawn/verdict set aside and case dismissed
Marijuana first possession One ounce or less marijuana / two grams or less THC; first offense; two-year rule
Marijuana summary pardon Misdemeanor possession, ingestion, paraphernalia; no convictions in past five years
BCI records Often not sealed by Chapter 12-60.1
Private background checks May require separate disputes after relief
This North Dakota reference is ready for Wilma training, intake triage, eligibility screening, packet preparation, and escalation logic.
